dataset: historical_eclipses.json (NASA GSFC Five Millennium Canon of Solar Eclipses)

pi_items.source: Data file field 'name' = 'Arctic Eclipse'. NASA catalogs this as the 2026 Aug 12 total solar eclipse, Saros 126. 'Arctic Eclipse' is the standard informal name used by timeanddate.com and EarthSky.

Data file 'date' field. Confirmed by NASA GSFC eclipse catalog (https://eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov/SEpath/SEpath2001/SE2026Aug12Tpath.html) and timeanddate.com (https://www.timeanddate.com/eclipse/solar/2026-august-12).

Data file greatest_eclipse.time_utc. NASA GSFC path page gives 17:45:53.8 UT (Terrestrial Time); timeanddate.com gives 17:46:06 UTC. The ~12s difference is the Delta-T correction between TT and UTC. 17:46:06 UTC is the standard UTC value used by major reference sites.
Exact match with data file best_viewing_location.name for the 2026 eclipse entry. timeanddate.com confirms Reykjavik sees totality.
Matches data file best_viewing_location.latitude. Standard geographic coordinate for Reykjavik city center (Wikipedia: 64.1466 N).
Matches data file best_viewing_location.longitude. Standard geographic coordinate for Reykjavik city center (Wikipedia: 21.9426 W).
Computed: math.radians(64.1466) = 1.119569. Claimed 1.1198. Difference is 0.000231 rad (~0.013 degrees), well within the 0.10 rad tolerance used by all stellarium verifiers.
Computed: math.radians(-21.9426) = -0.382971. Claimed -0.3830. Difference is 0.000029 rad, negligible.
Matches data file best_viewing_location.altitude_m = 0. Reykjavik is a coastal city at sea level.
Standard geographic name. timeanddate.com confirms Italy sees only a 'Partial Solar Eclipse' from this event (7:24 pm to 8:46 pm CEST), validating the control observation design.
Rome city center coordinates from geographic databases: 41.9028 N, 12.4964 E. Claimed 41.90 is correct to 2 decimal places.
Rome city center: 12.4964 E. Claimed 12.50 is correct to 2 decimal places (difference 0.004 degrees = ~350m).
Computed: math.radians(41.90) = 0.731293. Claimed 0.7314. Difference 0.000107 rad, negligible.
Computed: math.radians(12.50) = 0.218166. Claimed 0.2181. Difference 0.000066 rad, negligible.

Matches data file best_viewing_location.totality_duration_sec = 80. Greatest eclipse duration is 132s (2m12s) at path center (65.12N, 25.23W). Reykjavik is offset from center, so 80s (~1m20s) is expected. NASA path width is 294km; Reykjavik is near the edge.

Task design parameters. Atmosphere ON for realistic eclipse rendering with corona and sky darkening. Landscape OFF for unobstructed horizon. Constellation lines ON for identifying objects during totality. Consistent with task description and standard eclipse observation practice.
Standard path pattern used by all stellarium tasks (e.g., titanic_sky_research.txt, session_notes.txt). Matches task description.
Default Stellarium screenshot directory configured in config.ini: screenshot_dir = /home/ga/Pictures/stellarium. Used consistently across all stellarium tasks.
Task description requires: 1 screenshot at each of 4 contacts (C1, C2, C3, C4) + 1 wide-field during totality + 1 Rome control = 6 minimum.

pi_items.verified_value: NASA Eclipse Database (eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov) - Five Millennium Canon of Solar Eclipses
pi_items.source: Data file _source field confirms 'NASA Eclipse Database (eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov)'. Cross-referenced against live NASA GSFC page at https://eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov/SEpath/SEpath2001/SE2026Aug12Tpath.html which provides the same eclipse parameters.

privileged_info_summary: The task uses real NASA eclipse data for the August 12, 2026 Arctic Total Solar Eclipse (Saros 126). The data file at /home/ga/data/historical_eclipses.json contains the 2026 eclipse entry with greatest eclipse at 17:46:06 UTC at coordinates 65.12N, 25.23W. The best viewing location is Reykjavik, Iceland (64.1466N, 21.9426W, 0m altitude) with 80 seconds of totality. The agent must set Stellarium to Reykjavik, configure atmosphere ON, landscape OFF, constellation lines ON, and find all four eclipse contact points (C1 partial begins, C2 totality begins, C3 totality ends, C4 partial ends) by visually advancing time from approximately one hour before greatest eclipse. The Sun altitude at mid-eclipse from Reykjavik will be approximately 20-25 degrees above the horizon (the Sun altitude at greatest eclipse point is 25.8 degrees per NASA). During totality, bright planets visible in the darkened sky should be identified. As a control observation, Rome Italy (41.90N, 12.50E) should show only a partial eclipse at mid-eclipse time, which is confirmed by timeanddate.com showing Italy receives only a partial eclipse. The report file must be written to ~/Desktop/eclipse_contact_times.txt containing all four contact UTC times with Sun altitude/azimuth, totality duration (C3 minus C2), visible objects during totality, and the Rome control result. A minimum of 6 screenshots are required (one per contact, one wide-field during totality, one from Rome).
